reviews.review_text: If you enjoy the feeling of your balls being placed in a hydraulic press, this game is a great fit for you!

I installed Rust at 3:14 AM and immediately my refrigerator started speaking Romanian. The loading screen lasted 8 years, but emotionally it felt like a Tuesday. First thing I did was punch a tree, which caused a nearby horse to file taxes under my name.

The gameplay is unbelievable. One minute you’re building a house, the next minute a naked guy named “Soup Dentist” hits you with a rock while reciting ancient microwave frequencies. I tried farming pumpkins but accidentally joined a maritime cult and became mayor of a wolf.

Graphics? Incredible. Every sunset looks like beef jerky flavored depression. The water physics are so realistic I almost drowned in my own keyboard. At one point I heard footsteps behind me in real life and immediately crafted three wooden doors and a furnace.

The community is extremely welcoming if you define “welcoming” as being called a dishwasher by a 9-year-old with military-grade audio equipment. I made one friend but he betrayed me for half a corn and a broken flashlight. Honestly fair trade.

10/10 experience. I no longer trust forests, metal fragments, or human civilization. My doctor says I have “Rust paranoia” and legally cannot own a sleeping bag anymore.
I play game I hit with rock I get wood THEN A BLACK GUY WITH A AK 47 LR ROCKET LAUNCHER BOOMS THE ♥♥♥♥ OUT OF ME AND SCREAMING RUSSIAN SLURS overall really enjoy the game.

Do not play this game if you have any hopes and dreams for your life. I am serious. This game will take everything from you. The wife... the kids... the house... even the dog... it's all gone.  Was it worth it? Maybe. I don't have the answer for that right now. I do know that I've spent over 6,900 hours of my life wasting away on this game. I haven't played a wipe in over 2 weeks and I'm still having Rust withdrawals. If you're a new player, you'll find out what that means soon enough. I've been apart of two different admin teams with 2 different orgs in the last 2 and 1/2 years and they both ended up a train wreck and ruined relationships. I've spent over 100$ on various in-game DLC over the years as well. Why did I spend 17.99$ recently on a bunch of skins for large boxes? Who knows. Maybe that's one of the reasons my wife took the kids. Anyways, I couldn't in good conscience recommend this game to any living human being. After all, this game has taken everything in my life from me. I remember nights where I would fall asleep with my headphones on full volume just so I would wake up and be able to hear if I'm getting raided... I think I might just be a psychopath but anyways... that's all I've got to say about Rust

Rust Review, 5,000 Hours Later

Rust is a survival game where you start naked on a beach with a rock, a torch, and a dream.

That dream usually lasts about 47 seconds before someone with an AK, night vision, and serious personal issues turns you into a loot bag.

I have spent 5,000 hours playing Rust. In that time, I have built bases, farmed stone, crafted weapons, made alliances, been betrayed, been raided, lost everything, rebuilt, got raided again, and somehow convinced myself that this time will be different.

It never is.

Rust is basically an abusive relationship with extra door codes.

You spend hours building the perfect base, carefully honeycombing it, adding traps, sorting boxes, upgrading walls, feeling like a genius. Then you log in the next day and find a hole where your house used to be and a sign outside saying “thanks for the loot”.

And yet, I keep coming back.

I die a lot.
I build a lot.
I get raided a lot.
I still have no idea how some 12-year-old with a bow has better aim than a trained sniper.

After 5,000 hours, I can confidently say I have not won Rust.

I am not even sure Rust can be won.

Rust is pain, paranoia, farming, betrayal, and waking up at 3am to check if your base still exists.

10/10, would lose everything again.

extremely toxic game where I'm stuck in a never-ending loop of building a base, get raided by better players, lose progress and then I rage quit in disgust .. only to log back in the next morning with somehow even more motivation to do it all over again

the game is addicting, the grind is addicting, people are extremely heartless 

but am I addicted ?

Rust used to be a game about freedom. If you didn't like the things someone was saying, you had a nice big mute button. The total chaos of it all made the game what it is, but now, they are implementing code that will scan and save your voice chats any time you talk in game which will then be analyzed by A.I. which will automatically ban you. This is some Orwellian ♥♥♥♥, and who knows what they're gonna do with the voice data. Sell it? Who knows. But it is clear, this is just another push for unwanted A.I. survlience and I'm positive this will lead to tons of false bans and cause the game to become of a shell of itself as everyone slowly stops using voice chat all together and instead uses 3rd party software to avoid those potential false bans, or simply because they aren't keen on some bot spying and analyzing everything  they say.. https://commits.facepunch.com/Jake_Rich/rust_reboot/main/hackweek_voice_moderation

A raid defence is 100% the most addictive part of this game as your in the fight for what feels like your life savings, only for it to wipe within a few days having you do the most exciting part of a wipe and that is fighting your way off the beach, finding a great place to call home and doing it all over again and again. No it doesn't get old. As soon as it feels like it's about to get mundane something wild happens to till the mundane hits again to somehow have the game totally twitch to addictive mode once more. I stopped playing Rust after repeated negative interactions with another player named Wyatt, a 17-year-old who did not have a job. Over time, his behavior became increasingly hostile and targeted, including constant harassment and attempts to ruin my experience in the game. What started as normal gameplay interactions turned into consistent bullying, making it difficult to enjoy the game at all. Eventually, the situation became frustrating enough that I decided to leave Rust entirely rather than continue dealing with the negativity.

I’ve played Rust for years and the new character model update significantly reduced my enjoyment of the game. I don’t like my new character and there isn’t currently a way to customize or change it. I’d reconsider my review if character customization is added.

Facepunch is slowly changing everything that made the old game enjoyable. The new character models are absolutely ghastly and have none of the charm the old models and animations had. Not to mention the inclusion of handholding BS like team ID system, compass, live map showing all POIs, server event notifications and endless hints at how to play because modern gamers apparently can't figure out anything themselves.
I started playing in 2016 and the sense of isolation/survival in a huge, post-apocolyptic world was one thing that made Rust special for me. There's none of that feeling now when you can just stop by an NPC shop and buy a horse, minicopter or gun. 
There weren't any hints, you had to discover and explore things yourself. Not much point now with a live map that shows everything at once in real time. I think many of these changes were to appease big streamers who like to put their fists through their expensive keyboards when they die.
It seems as though Facepunch doesn't know what direction to take this game anymore, so they're turning it into a weird mashup of mostly Rust with elements taken from Tarkov, Pubg and Scum.

Bought this game after watching streamers play it. Thought "yeah I can do that." I cannot do that.

Joined a PvE server to "learn the mechanics." One hundred hours later I have learned exactly one mechanic: how to take photographs.

My base is no longer a base. It is an a gallery. It is called Kojima's Gallery and it is, exclusively, feet pics. Every wall. Every floor. Every ceiling tile. I have curated this collection with the obsessive precision of a person who has clearly lost the plot somewhere between gathering wood and finding their calling.

People come from across the server to visit. Some leave reviews. One guy left and didn't speak to me for, well he still hasn't. 

A new player wandered in looking for loot, only to find my vast collection of people's feet along my walls and left a changed man. 

I do not know what a Bradley is, havent had a moment with a attack helicopter, or even really explore the monuments.

But the one thing I have, is lighting. Mood lighting. For the feet. Maybe even your feet if youre lucky enough.

Sometimes I wonder what I could have been. A raider. A builder. A farmer with sprawling greenhouses.

Instead I am a someone with a vision, and that vision is toes.

Hideo would be proud. Probably. Hopefully.

Rust is great!
Pros:

Fun survival mechanics!

Cons:

Players that have no life

Any noise that you make is going to get you killed 

Progression is broken

Recoil has is now horrendous

You will have all of your work stripped away unless you have the rust app or a large and lifeless team

The game runs like hot ass regardless of what operating system you use

You need a high tier cpu to run the game at a reasonable frame rate 

Linux is unsupported due to Facepunches ignorance
   - IF you decide to play on Linux, you can only play a handful of servers, most of which are full of cheaters and other poor bastards that cannot play any other way without setting up their own server

Many cosmetics are pay to win

This game will beat you down at every opportunity it’s given.

The game is overrated and overpriced.

Cheaters are in every server, regardless of anti cheat.

PvE is horrible, the NPCs are notoriously terrible to combat for newer players, older seasoned players just glitch them or exploit their overly aggressive AI.

PvP is worse, sometimes you win a fight and get a load of high tier loot just to get one tapped by Bobby on his roof. Otherwise, your neighbor moves his fat ass out of his base to door camp you and go deep within your base.

Raiding is a mechanic that is fun, but once it happens to you it’s the worst thing imaginable.

Wipes are the only glimmer of hope small groups/solos have of actually achieving something on the server. If you miss a wipe, consider yourself primlocked and offline raided the next day.

Offline raiders have all the low risk high reward, there is no protection against them that cannot be countered.

Overall: 6/10

Rust is genuinely one of the greatest survival games ever made, and I do not say that lightly. This game is pure chaos, pure pain, pure comedy, and pure addiction all wrapped into one brutal masterpiece. There is no other game that can make you feel like an unstoppable warlord one minute and a homeless caveman begging for mercy the next. Rust is not just a game you play. Rust is a full psychological experience.

The best thing about Rust is that nothing is ever guaranteed. You can spawn on the beach with nothing but a rock and a torch, get chased by a naked man screaming into his mic, somehow survive, farm enough materials to build a starter base, and then two hours later you are deep in a snow biome fighting full metal AK players over an airdrop. Every single wipe feels like a completely different story. Some wipes you snowball instantly. Some wipes you get doorcamped by the most unemployed human beings on Earth. Some wipes you become the villain. Some wipes the server makes you the victim. That is what makes it so good.

The progression in Rust is insanely satisfying. Going from a stone hatchet and bow to furnaces, guns, explosives, electricity, turrets, compounds, and raids feels better than almost any progression system in gaming. Every item matters because you worked for it. Every gun feels valuable because you either farmed for it, crafted it, stole it, or risked everything to bring it home. Getting your first SAR, Thompson, or AK of the wipe feels like winning the lottery. Getting back to base with a full inventory of farm while hearing footsteps nearby is more intense than most horror games.

The PvP is another reason Rust is so legendary. The fights are messy, stressful, and rewarding. Positioning matters. Aim matters. Game sense matters. Confidence matters. Even with all the chaos, there is a real skill gap. You can tell when someone knows how to move, when to peek, when to fall back, when to bait, when to push, and when to just run for their life. Every fight feels personal because there is actual risk. You are not just losing a round. You are losing the kit you farmed, crafted, or stole. That makes every kill feel amazing and every death feel like a crime against humanity.

Base building is honestly one of the most underrated parts of the game. Rust lets you be creative in a way most survival games do not. You can make a tiny 2x1 shack, a bunker base, a cave base, a clan compound, a trap base, a roleplayer mansion, or the most disgusting honeycombed fortress known to man. There is so much depth in building that it almost feels like its own separate game. Learning how to protect loot, place doors correctly, hide TC, design peaks, add external TCs, set up turrets, and make your base annoying to raid is genuinely fun. Nothing feels better than logging on and seeing your base survived the night.

The social side of Rust is what makes it completely unmatched. This game has some of the funniest, weirdest, most toxic, and most memorable interactions in gaming. You will meet random nakeds who become allies for the whole wipe. You will meet neighbors who act friendly and then betray you the second they get a chance. You will meet clans that treat a video game wipe like a military operation. You will hear the most unhinged voice chat imaginable. Rust creates stories naturally. You do not need scripted missions because the players are the content.

Raiding is one of the most adrenaline-filled things in any game. The moment you start throwing satchels, rockets, or C4, everything changes. You hear counters coming. You hear doors opening. You wonder if the base owner is online. You wonder if you brought enough boom. You wonder if the loot is going to be insane or if you just wasted hours raiding a base full of pumpkins and waterpipes. The uncertainty is what makes it so good. A successful raid can change your entire wipe. A failed raid can ruin your night. That risk is exactly why it is exciting.

Rust also keeps getting better because the game is constantly evolving. The map, monuments, weapons, vehicles, events, boats, trains, oil rigs, labs, cargo, helicopters, electricity, farming, industrial systems, and so many other features give players endless ways to play. You can be a PvPer, farmer, builder, electrician, fisherman, roleplayer, trap base goblin, ocean rat, monument runner, clan leader, solo grub, or just a menace to society. The game gives you tools and lets you decide what kind of creature you want to become.

And honestly, Rust looks and feels way better than people give it credit for. The environments can be beautiful, especially during sunrise or sunset. Snow biomes, forests, deserts, beaches, and monuments all have their own vibe. Then five seconds later someone is shooting at you from a bush and the beauty turns into panic. That contrast is perfect. Rust can be peaceful when you are farming wood at night with the sound of the ocean nearby, and then instantly turn into a warzone because a naked with an eoka decided your existence was offensive.

Is Rust frustrating? Absolutely. Is it toxic? Definitely. Will you lose hours of progress to the dumbest thing imaginable? Yes. Will you rage? Yes. Will you say you are done with the game forever and then load it back up the next day? Also yes. But that is exactly what makes Rust special. The highs are so high because the lows are brutal. The game does not hold your hand, and that is why every win feels earned.

Rust is not for everyone, but for the people who understand it, there is nothing else like it. It is one of the only games where every wipe feels like a story you actually lived through. You remember the base you built, the neighbors you hated, the raid you barely won, the kit you lost, the snowball that changed everything, and the random guy who betrayed you after you gave him a bag. No other game creates memories like Rust.

Overall, Rust is a masterpiece of survival, PvP, betrayal, creativity, and pure chaos. It is painful, hilarious, stressful, rewarding, and insanely addictive. It is the kind of game that can ruin your mood and still make you call it peak gaming. Rust is not just good. Rust is legendary.

I find it unusual that this game and Game Dev think I played this game at all. I was marginally interested in at least checking this out, but for some reason, despite the fact I've never played this game (nor installed it for that matter), I now have an unexplainable perma-ban? OK then.

I come back to this game after a years break and i gotta say this game has went downhill, off a cliff, and exploded. Whoever thought it was a good idea to make blueprint fragments needs to get molly raided irl. The playstyle has now become if you don't do card swipes you don't progress. The least fun game mechanic is now REQUIRED. The strat has now become just camp the monuments and steal cards off of people and clans can primlock the server. The performance is actually horrible. I'm getting 60 frames on a good day and i've got a 40 series. Night time is still just 15 minutes of unplayable boredom and if you dare exit your base be prepared to be grubbed. Rust is the type of game that you will really enjoy if you have no life outside of a computer screen. If you can dedicate 8+ hours of your time every single day, you will be good at rust, and have a good time. If you have a life outside of rust however, with the responsibilities that follow? Do not buy this game. Do not waste your time on this game. You can spend all of your free time playing this game, and never be as good, or have as much stuff as the 17 year old greaseball neckbeard who plays this game like a job.

 Another reason you might want to avoid this game, is the fact that the player base is easily the most toxic player base you will ever come across, in any video game. You can simply say you don't like something about a specific server, and 15 random teenagers with no real life outside of this game, will tell you to kill your self, and say that your children deserve death. This is not a joke. These "people" will sit on their roofs for hours upon hours, making sure that everything that moves is killed, even if they do not gain anything from it. They will sit outside of your door for hours, simply because you killed them once, or said something they didn't like. Steal something from the wrong clan, just trying to enjoy a game after work? Be prepared for a 10 man clan to place TC's  around your entire base, with auto turrets covering each of them, all of which prevent you from playing out of that base entirely.

Lastly, FacePunch caters this entire game to people who play with groups. If you do not play with a group, you are pretty much entirely screwed, unless you are willing to spend hours just grinding out stuff. This game is not meant for solo play, because everyone plays as a team, and FacePunch very obviously cares about the team's more than the individual players. The current progression system is not fair to solo players whatsoever, and if you try to play solo or in a small group with inexperienced teammates, your wipe is pretty much over from the start.

With all of this being said, as someone who once loved rust, I highly recommend that you DO NOT buy this game, or spend your life trying to enjoy it. Unless you are just as toxic as the rest of this community, and have no life outside of video games, this game is not for you. The developers don't care about you, the player base genuinely hates you, and most staff don't do ♥♥♥♥. On top of everything else that I said, there are cheaters pretty much everywhere, on nearly every server. Find one that is breaking rules but being clever about it? 9/10 times, they will not be banned.

If this prevents even one person from buying this game, it will have been worth the time I spent writing this review. It may be too late to return this game, but if I could, I would. I don't think I will ever play this game again, in its current state.

The cheating problem in this game has gotten out of control lately and it's seriously hurting the enjoyment for a lot of players.
When I lose my base, I want it to happen because someone actually raided me fairly — preferably while I'm offline. Instead, we're constantly dealing with hackers phasing straight into bases, destroying everything, and headshotting players through walls. It's not fun, it's not fair, and it's driving people away.
Please prioritize anti-cheat improvements. The current situation is making the game feel unplayable for legitimate players.

I bought Rust only 7 days ago, and today I suddenly found that my account has been game banned without any clear reason.

I was not even playing the game today — in fact, I had not opened Rust for a full day before seeing this ban. I am confused because I did not receive any explanation.

During the time I played, I never used cheats, hacks, scripts, or any third-party software. I honestly do not even know how to use cheats. I only played the game normally.

Edit: 

Even the customer support from the game studio has been very disappointing.

I submitted a ticket asking for an explanation for the ban because I did not do anything wrong. Their response was simply that I was permanently banned, without explaining the reason.

Every time I open a new ticket asking them to review my account or explain what happened, the ticket gets closed almost immediately.

At this point, I do not know what to do. I still do not understand why I was banned, and no one is willing to explain or properly review my account. I am only asking for a fair check, because I am certain I did not cheat.

Rust is one of those games that absolutely consumes your life — and not in a good way. It starts out fun: you gather wood, build a base, meet (and usually get betrayed by) other players. But before you realize it, you’re stuck in a vicious cycle of grinding, paranoia, and sleepless nights worrying if your base will still be standing when you log back in. The constant fear of being raided and the endless need to rebuild can feel less like playing a game and more like maintaining a second life. It’s incredibly addictive to the point of being crippling — you keep coming back even though it stops being fun long ago. Rust doesn’t just eat your time; it eats your peace of mind.

A game for the masochists that enjoy being killed by sweats every 5 minutes. Almost impenetrable for the new player if playing on Vanilla official with little or no quarter given for newbies. I can see the potential for those that reach a certain level but there must be thousands of players like me that are not prepared to die 10 thousand times before gaining a proper foothold. In summary not for the casual player, diehards only.

Rust is a soul-sucking nightmare wrapped in pixels. Every second feels like a personal assault on your patience. Tedious, punishing, and absurdly cruel, it drains your sanity while laughing in your face. A monument to chaos and misery, it doesn’t just challenge you it hunts you down and mocks you for existing.

The game is unnecessarily difficult, especially for beginners. The resource balance is terrible: cloth, which is essential for crafting basic items like sleeping bags, is almost impossible to find early on, completely blocking progression.
The game offers no real exploration or adventure; it just turns into a toxic PvP experience where the only goal is to kill other players on sight. If you're looking for an immersive survival experience, you'll be disappointed by the lack of PvE content.
On top of that, creating a private server to play peacefully with friends is a technical nightmare. Rust is only suitable for players who want an ultra-competitive experience and have hours to waste every day. If you're looking for adventure and relaxation, stay far away.

SAVE YOUR MONEY!

I went back to play Rust after not playing for years. It’s still a fun game at its core, and I managed to put around 180 hours into it. It would honestly still be worth playing in 2026 with all the updates and improved graphics — BUT… that’s only if it wasn’t full of cheaters.

The game feels like it’s filled with DMA cheaters with no lives, playing 24/7 using full wall hacks and aimbot. (Seriously, it feels like 80% of lobbies are cheating) — even on premium servers.

It’s worse than the cheating issues in CS:GO/CS2 because there is basically no counterplay. And The report system is completely useless.

I’ve never seen so many instant onetap headshots while:

Running
On a horse
Driving a car
all from 100+ meters away with any weapon

You’ll also get wiped by a duo or group that suddenly shows up crouched, pre-aimed, and fully aware of your position right after you’ve been looting scrap for 30 minutes. They were nowhere to be seen when you were empty, but the moment you get scrap or decent gear they magically appear — sneaking in from nowhere already pre-aimed, or rolling up in a car as a 2–3 man just to instantly kill you and farm nakeds or anyone with loot or components.

Most of them also seem to be cheating in groups or full clans. I’ve only seen a handful of suspicious solo players with the same ridiculous aim and knowledge of exactly where you’re about to peek.

And this isn’t a one-off thing. It happened repeatedly — well over 100+ times — to the point where it wasn’t just bad luck or coincidence. Even across different loot runs, hours apart, and over multiple days.

You’ll be playing normally with no one around, carefully watching your surroundings, gather a few hundred scrap and components, and suddenly before you can even think about going to a recycler or back to base a car shows up out of nowhere. They weren’t driving past or scouting — they already seem to know exactly where you are. They pre-fire you, kill you instantly, take everything, and the cycle repeats. Its just blatant ESP/wall hacks and full information awareness every single time.

Out of my 180 hours, I played on one premium server end of wipe and at a fresh wipe:

Server 1 ( premium around 5–6 days before wipe, 60–80 pop): This one was actually fun and challenging. I had a real progression experience here. By day 4 I had already hit end game, had all unlocks, and was raiding. I only died early on about 5 times to a suspicious 4-man group that door camped me once I had a lot of farm. After that, I choose a new location dominated for the next few days with 30+ kills, raided bases, fought solos and duos, and won every single fight. After that initial group, I didn’t really run into any obviously suspicious players, and it felt like how Rust should be — fights over airdrops, elite crates, and PvP progression and doing monuments.

Server 2 (same premium after fresh wipe): Completely unplayable. It felt like it was flooded with cheaters from the start. You couldn’t progress at all past level 1 workbench without being constantly tracked, killed, and farmed. Eventually the server just died out as everyone quit and the cheaters left bases to decay from boredom and waiting for next wipe.

If you want to play, your only real options are:

Play with cheaters using DMA cheats yourself, or
Wait until the last 5-7 days before wipe when things calm down a bit and hope you don’t run into blatant cheaters every few minutes with nothing better to do with thier lives as most of they will have gotten bored and left server before it wipes.

I don't hate myself enough to allow a bunch of arrogant, entitled devs tell me what gender my avatar should be in their half-baked cash cow excuse of a game.

Played solo for a bit on a self-hosted server. Then they kept force-wiping it every day. There isn't even a game anymore.

I’ve got 7,600 hours on Rust and I enjoy the game overall, but trying to get new players into it has been brutal lately.

My friend literally just bought the game, barely got started, and was getting some weird “not capable” type error. Then the next day he suddenly got hit with a game ban out of nowhere.

He submitted a support ticket and basically got no explanation. They just said it wasn’t their fault and gave no real reason why the ban happened. He kept asking what triggered it and never got an actual answer.

He doesn’t cheat, doesn’t use overlays, never had his Steam account hacked, and had only just started playing. Getting permanently banned with zero explanation is a terrible experience for a new player.

Rust is already hard enough for beginners without random bans making people quit before they even start.
